options: The value of x that minimizes f(x)
The interval where f(x) is negative
questions.correctAnswer: The value of x that minimizes f(x)
questions.explanation: Argmin refers to the argument, meaning the input value x at which the function reaches its minimum, not the minimum function value itself.

questions.prompt: For an interior optimum x*, the standard first-order condition is:
questions.explanation: At an interior stationary point, the slope must vanish. That is why optimization can be reframed as solving the equation f'(x)=0.

questions.prompt: Why is optimization presented as an extension of equation solving in this lesson?
options: Because you solve f'(x)=0 to locate stationary points
Because every optimization problem is linear
Because the objective function must be zero
questions.correctAnswer: Because you solve f'(x)=0 to locate stationary points
questions.explanation: The lecture's core bridge is that minima and maxima are found by setting the derivative to zero, which turns optimization into a root-finding problem on f'(x).

questions.prompt: If f''(x*)>0 at a stationary point, the point is typically a:
questions.explanation: Positive second derivative means the graph bends upward near x*, so the stationary point behaves like the bottom of a bowl and is therefore a local minimum.

questions.prompt: If f''(x*)<0 at a stationary point, the point is typically a:
questions.explanation: Negative second derivative means the graph bends downward near x*, so the point behaves like the top of a hill and is therefore a local maximum.

questions.prompt: What is the most important difference between a stationary point and a guaranteed optimum?
options: A stationary point only satisfies f'(x)=0, but still needs classification
A stationary point always has f(x)=0
questions.correctAnswer: A stationary point only satisfies f'(x)=0, but still needs classification
questions.explanation: Zero derivative identifies a critical point, but you still need curvature or endpoint comparison to determine whether it is a minimum, a maximum, or something else.

questions.prompt: Why must endpoints also be checked on a closed interval [a,b]?
options: Because derivatives are undefined there by definition
Because the global optimum can occur at an endpoint
Because stationary points cannot exist inside the interval
Because numerical methods only work at endpoints
questions.correctAnswer: Because the global optimum can occur at an endpoint
questions.explanation: Even if interior stationary points exist, the smallest or largest function value over a closed interval may still occur at a boundary point, so endpoints must be compared.

questions.prompt: Near an optimum x*, the derivative f'(x_k) is mainly influenced by:
questions.explanation: The Taylor expansion in the lecture shows that near x*, the derivative behaves primarily like (x_k-x*)f''(x*), so curvature controls the local behavior of the optimization updates.

questions.prompt: If you are minimizing and f'(x_k)>0, the next move should be generally toward:
questions.explanation: Positive derivative means the function increases as x increases, so to go downhill toward a minimum you should move in the negative-x direction.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the big idea of this concept?
options: Optimization and equation solving are unrelated topics
Optimization becomes root-finding when you work with the derivative
Only maxima can be found numerically
questions.correctAnswer: Optimization becomes root-finding when you work with the derivative
questions.explanation: That is the central message of the lecture's opening section: by solving f'(x)=0, optimization problems inherit the logic and methods of equation solving.
